Title: Oxidative Rearrangements of the Alkaloid Intermediate Geissoschizine

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch-point oxidative cyclization from central intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively transforms 19E-geissoschizine to forge the C7-C16 bond, producing the akuammiline-type scaffold rhazimal, which can deformylate to strictamine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate precursor of target product and branch-defining scaffold
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Monoterpene indole alkaloids with pharmacological and natural product relevance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes formation of akuammiline-type alkaloid scaffold from geissoschizine; enables production of rhazimal-derived alkaloids in C. roseus
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in Saccharomyces cerevisiae microsomes; in vitro enzyme assay with geissoschizine; product isolation and NMR; metabolite profiling in C. roseus tissues; site-directed mutagenesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro microsomal assays; heterologous yeast expression
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome mining/phylogeny of C. roseus CYP71 genes; accession not provided
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering akuammiline-type alkaloid scaffold production from geissoschizine in heterologous or plant systems
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In planta function could not be definitively established by VIGS because CrRS is root-expressed while silencing was only feasible in leaf and stem tissue; rhazimal readily deformylates to strictamine
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CrRS was identified from C. roseus as a CYP71 enzyme that converts 19E-geissoschizine to rhazimal. 2. The product defines the akuammiline-type branch via C7-C16 bond formation and can deformylate to strictamine. 3. C. roseus roots contain the rhazimal-derived alkaloid akuammiline, supporting physiological relevance. 4. Mutations in CrRS can switch product specificity toward CrGO- or CrSBE-like activities.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch-point oxidative cyclization/oxidation from central intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes 19E-geissoschizine at the C5-N4 region to form an iminium intermediate; this undergoes Mannich cyclization to polyneuridine aldehyde or further oxidation to tetradehydrogeissoschizine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms branch-defining scaffold and competing oxidized product
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Monoterpene indole alkaloids with pharmacological and natural product relevance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces sarpagan-type alkaloid intermediate polyneuridine aldehyde from geissoschizine, but also generates tetradehydrogeissoschizine through overoxidation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in Saccharomyces cerevisiae microsomes; in vitro enzyme assay; coelution with standards; coupled enzyme assays with RsPNAE and RsVS; VIGS in C. roseus; site-directed mutagenesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Product distribution depends on time in vitro and presence of downstream enzymes that capture polyneuridine aldehyde
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome mining/phylogeny of C. roseus CYP71 genes; accession not provided
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Physiological function in C. roseus remains unknown; overoxidation diverts flux to tetradehydrogeissoschizine
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be combined with downstream enzymes such as PNAE and VS to channel flux toward stable sarpagan-derived products like vinorine
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Polyneuridine aldehyde appears initially and can revert/convert over time to tetradehydrogeissoschizine; silencing did not reduce serpentine/alstonine, leaving native role unresolved
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CrSBE was identified as a C. roseus CYP71 enzyme acting on 19E-geissoschizine. 2. It forms polyneuridine aldehyde via oxidative activation and Mannich cyclization, but the product is reversible and can convert to tetradehydrogeissoschizine. 3. Addition of RsPNAE and RsVS captures the intermediate and yields vinorine. 4. Its physiological role in C. roseus is still unclear.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch-point oxidative cyclization from central intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Dehydropreakuammicine; minor 16-formyl-pleiocarpamine inferred, observed as 16-epi-pleiocarpamine after deformylation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively converts 19E-geissoschizine to dehydropreakuammicine through C16-C2 bond formation; also forms a minor mavacurane-pathway product via C16-N1 bond formation leading to pleiocarpamine/16-epi-pleiocarpamine after deformylation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target scaffold and also produces minor side-product branch contributing to mavacurane alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Monoterpene indole alkaloids with pharmacological and natural product relevance
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes the canonical strychnos-type scaffold formation and also contributes to mavacurane-type alkaloid biosynthesis as a minor but physiologically relevant side activity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro microsomal enzyme assays; product comparison to authentic standards and MS2; transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana leaf discs fed geissoschizine; VIGS in C. roseus with metabolite profiling; mutational analysis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro versus in planta product distributions differ; transient expression tested in Nicotiana benthamiana leaves
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Produces minor side products, indicating split flux among alkaloid scaffolds
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising enzyme for engineering both strychnos-type and mavacurane-type alkaloid scaffolds from a common precursor
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Silencing CrGO significantly reduced pleiocarpamine and 16-epi-pleiocarpamine in C. roseus leaves, supporting physiological relevance of the minor side product route
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CrGO catalyzes oxidative rearrangement of geissoschizine to the strychnos-type product dehydropreakuammicine. 2. CrGO also generates minor mavacurane-type products leading to 16-epi-pleiocarpamine and likely pleiocarpamine. 3. VIGS of CrGO reduced pleiocarpamine and 16-epi-pleiocarpamine in planta, showing that minor side activity is biologically relevant. 4. Mutational analysis indicates product selectivity is controlled by a small number of active-site residues.
